sections.title: Living in Harmony
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens with images of animals, birds, insects, and plants that share human living spaces — a myna, hibiscus, dragonfly, cat, snail, goat, grasshopper, and potted plants. Students are asked which of these live in their homes and whether their families care for them. The section establishes the idea that some animals and plants are invited into homes while others arrive uninvited, and explores human feelings and responses to both. Students discuss with elders which animals and plants their families kept when they were young, building intergenerational cultural awareness. A Find out task asks students to identify animals they have spotted that are not shown in the pictures, note their names, and record where they were seen.

tasks.task_text: Do any of these animals, birds, insects and plants live with you in your house?
Do you and your family take care of them?
Are there any animals, birds and insects that you have spotted that are not in the picture given on page 72? Try to find out their names and write them in the table given below.
Which are the uninvited animals, birds and insects that visit your home?
Why do you think they come to your homes?
How do you feel about their presence in your home?
Which of them do you like?
What do you do when you do not like them?

sections.title: The Mango Tree
sections.content_summary: Through the story of Shambhu and his father sitting under a mango tree in the forest, this section illustrates how a single tree serves as home to birds, squirrels, monkeys, and many other animals. The key ecological insight — delivered by the father — is that when a monkey eats a mango and throws the seed, a new plant can emerge: animals help plants spread. The section presents a radial diagram of uses of plants: shade, wood, medicine, fibre, fruit, fresh air, and shelter for animals — connecting natural ecology to human material and cultural life. Students discuss the five-way interdependence between humans, plants, and animals.

tasks.task_text: How are we dependent on plants and animals?
How do plants help animals?
How do animals help plants?
How do you look after plants and animals in your surroundings?
How do animals and plants depend on us?

sections.title: We Need Each Other
sections.content_summary: This section develops the concept of mutual dependence: humans provide care, protection, food and shelter to animals and plants, and in return receive food (milk from cows, buffaloes, and goats), shelter material, clothing fibre, and companionship. Animals help plants by dispersing seeds and making soil fertile through their waste, enabling plants to grow and spread. A 'Do you know?' panel highlights the accidental tree planting by squirrels who bury and forget nuts — a playful illustration of unintentional ecological service. Activity 1 asks students to choose an animal they see regularly and observe it closely over time, recording its behaviour, movement, and sounds. Activity 2 is the 'Busy, Busy, Clever Ants' experiment: students place three types of food (sweet, fried, cooked) on the floor and predict and observe ant behaviour and preferences.

tasks.task_text: Get to Know an Animal. Observe the animals around you. Choose any animal that you see often, such as a goat, cow, dog, cat or bird like a crow, pigeon, sparrow, parrot or duck. Observe it closely whenever you get a chance. You can also keep a bowl of water and some grains for it.

Write a short description of the animal or bird — Name and describe the animal or bird that you have chosen. When and where did you first see it? Was it there alone or with its friends? Describe its movement. What sounds did it make? Did you see it eating, sleeping, talking to its friends or maybe fighting sometimes? Did it do anything that surprised you or made you laugh? Share your story with your classmates.

Busy, Busy, Busy and Clever Ants! Bring small amounts of three types of food from your kitchen: Sweet like sugar or jaggery; Fried like a papad or murukku; Boiled or cooked like bread, chapati, idli, rice or ragi. Keep these three types of food on the floor in a straight line with one foot distance between them. Make a Guess — Who will come for the food? Which will be their favourite food? Will any ants come to your food? Where would they come from? Will all the ants look the same? What food item will they go to first? How many ants will come to the food? What will they do with the food?

sections.title: Visiting your Plant Friend
sections.content_summary: This section introduces an emotionally grounded human-nature practice: visiting one's plant friend when feeling sad, upset, angry, or happy, and talking to it about one's feelings. It frames plants as needing care and support just as humans do — establishing empathy and reciprocity as the basis of harmony. Activity 3 ('Guess Who I Am?') is a pair game where students mime the movement of an animal, bird, insect, or tree for their class to guess — connecting bodily expression and imaginative play to knowledge of living things. The section closes with the statement: 'Plants also need our care and support. So do animals. We can all help each other.' A 'Do you know?' panel shares the true story of Valparai, Tamil Nadu, where school children and teachers track and celebrate the arrival of migratory Grey Wagtails (Vaalatti kuruvi in Tamil) each winter with welcome posters and sweets — an example of community-level cultural harmony with nature.

tasks.task_text: Guess Who I Am? Sit in pairs. One of you will think of the name of an animal, bird, insect or tree and whisper it in the ear of your partner. Your partner will have to act and move like the animal, bird or insect and the class will have to guess it! You can switch roles after this.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The closing reflection consolidates the chapter's themes of interdependence, care, and compassion. Discussion questions prompt students to articulate human dependence on plants and animals and the responsibility to care for them. Writing tasks ask students to complete sentence frames expressing personal connection to living beings they love, and to rank all the animals they have encountered from smallest to largest. The Draw task asks students to name and draw animals, birds, and insects categorised by number of legs — two, four, six, and eight — building classification skills rooted in personal observation.

tasks.task_text: How are we dependent on plants and animals?
How should we care for plants and animals in our environment?
How do plants and animals depend on us?

Complete these sentences with the names of plants, birds, animals or insects: a. I like looking at _____________ because _____________. b. ________ make me laugh because ____________. c. My friend _________________ likes ______________ because _____________________________. d. I would like to take care of _______________ because ____________________________.

a. Which is your favourite animal, bird, insect or plant among those you have named above? b. Why do you like it? c. Write down the names of all the animals, birds, insects that you have seen or learned about from the smallest to the largest.
Name and draw animals, birds and insects that have: a. Two legs, b. Four legs, c. Six legs, d. Eight legs.

dual_strand.natural: Mutual dependence between plants, animals, and humans; seed dispersal and soil fertility through animal activity; animal behaviour and habitat
dual_strand.human_cultural: Human care, compassion, and responsibility toward all living beings; cultural celebration of nature such as the Valparai Grey Wagtail welcome tradition; intergenerational transmission of knowledge about animals and plants kept at home
